title: San Francisco homeowner fined $50K for trimming street trees
author: Ellsworth Toohey
summary: Paul Dennes got an email from his insurance broker: trim the trees near your San Francisco home, or you won't qualify for coverage. He trimmed them. Then the city hit him with a $50,000 fine, according to ABC7.
full_text:
Paul Dennes got an email from his insurance broker: trim the trees near your San Francisco home, or you won't qualify for coverage. He trimmed them. Then the city hit him with a $50,000 fine, according to ABC7.

The five trees in front of Dennes's Panhandle neighborhood unit look like his trees — they're planted in the sidewalk directly outside the building his family has owned since 1988. He says he'd never seen the city trim them. But in 2017, San Francisco voters passed Proposition E, transferring responsibility for all public street trees to the Bureau of Urban Forestry and allocating more than $19 million a year to maintain them. The trees, sitting in the public right-of-way, became city property. Dennes didn't know. (The city's outreach strategy was its website. Dennes's father, the primary owner, is 94 years old. "He didn't look at websites!" Dennes told ABC7.)

The city charged $10,000 per tree for "topping" — cutting main branches back to stubs, which can shock trees and invite decay. A neighbor had reportedly warned Dennes against the method and suggested using a certified arborist; a certified arborist is apparently the one who then reported him. After an appeal, a second inspector revised the assessment: the trees might survive, though with shortened lifespans and weakened structure. The fine dropped to $12,950.

This week, SF Public Works told ABC7 it would further reduce the fine to $6,475 — half of the revised amount — if Dennes agrees to hire a certified arborist to properly prune all five trees over the next five years. "I didn't know. It doesn't feel fair at all," Dennes said, according to ABC7.
